evals.prompt: I'm trying to document our payment processing system. Here's what we have: a React frontend where customers check out, a Node.js API that handles order logic, a PostgreSQL database for orders and inventory, and we integrate with Stripe for payments and SendGrid for order confirmation emails. Can you create a C4 container diagram for this? Use Structurizr DSL.
evals.expected_output: A valid Structurizr DSL workspace with a container diagram showing the payment system's internal containers (React frontend, Node.js API, PostgreSQL database) and external systems (Stripe, SendGrid), with specific relationship labels and technology annotations.
expectations: Output contains a Structurizr DSL workspace block with model and views sections (verify by structure: workspace { model { ... } views { ... } })
The model defines a person representing the customer
The model defines containers for the React frontend, Node.js API, and PostgreSQL database — each with technology specified in the technology slot of the container declaration
Stripe and SendGrid are modeled as external software systems at the workspace/model level, not as containers inside the payment system softwareSystem block
Relationship labels are specific verb phrases describing what flows — none of the labels is the bare word 'Uses', 'Reads', 'Writes', or 'Connects'
Inter-container relationship declarations include a technology/protocol value in the second string slot (e.g., 'JSON/HTTPS', 'JDBC', 'REST') — empty or missing protocol slot fails this assertion
The views section includes a container view scoped to the payment system softwareSystem
Either the workspace name, the softwareSystem name, or the container view's description contains both the word 'Container' and the system name
The output includes a styles block OR a comment/note explaining how to render the DSL (e.g., paste into structurizr.com/dsl)

evals.prompt: We're designing a new internal tool for our company. It's a support ticket system — support agents will use a web app to manage tickets, customers will submit tickets through our public website and via email. We want to use a message queue to decouple the ingestion from processing. We'll need a database and probably a notification service to alert agents of new high-priority tickets. Help me think through the architecture. Draw C4 diagrams at the right levels to work through this design.
evals.expected_output: C4 diagrams at multiple levels (at minimum system context and container) that help work through the design, with proper notation, element labeling, and specific relationship descriptions.
expectations: A System Context diagram is produced showing the ticket system, its users (support agents, customers), and any external systems
A Container diagram is produced showing the internal architecture (web app, message queue, database, notification service, and any other containers identified)
Each diagram has a title stating its type and scope
People are distinguished by role — support agents and customers are separate, not collapsed into a generic 'User'

Every container has a concrete technology suggestion (a specific named tech like 'Node.js', 'Postgres', 'RabbitMQ' — role descriptors like 'background worker process' do not satisfy this; disjunctions like 'RabbitMQ / SQS' do)
The message queue is represented as a container with a named queue technology
Relationship labels are specific verb phrases describing what flows — none of the labels is the bare word 'Uses', 'Reads', 'Writes', or 'Connects'

Both submission channels mentioned in the prompt (web form AND email) have a complete end-to-end path on the diagrams from the customer all the way to the ticket database — neither is dropped or implied
Arrow direction matches the verb in each label (e.g., 'polls X from' arrow points from poller to source; 'publishes events to' arrow points from publisher to broker)

Container technology choices are coherent with their stated relationships (e.g., a Node.js container does not have a JDBC arrow to a database; a Python container does not have an Express component; cross-stack contradictions fail this assertion)

If the diagrams use both prose narration and visual arrows for the same flow, the prose and the arrows agree about who initiates and who responds (e.g., prose saying 'Email Provider delivers' must not pair with an arrow labeled 'polls inbound email from')

evals.prompt: Can you create a Mermaid C4 diagram of our microservice setup? We have an API Gateway (Kong), three backend services: User Service (Go), Order Service (Java/Spring Boot), and Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI). Each service has its own database — User has PostgreSQL, Order has MySQL, Inventory has MongoDB. Services communicate via gRPC. There's also a RabbitMQ message broker for async events between services. The whole thing is used by a mobile app and a web app.
evals.expected_output: A valid Mermaid C4Container diagram with correct syntax, all containers with technology labels, boundaries, and specific relationship labels including protocols.
expectations: Output uses the C4Container Mermaid diagram type (the first non-comment line is `C4Container`)

All entities mentioned in the prompt are represented: API Gateway, User Service, Order Service, Inventory Service, and the mobile and web apps — modeled either as containers inside the system boundary or as external actors, either is acceptable
Each database is represented with ContainerDb (not plain Container) — User Service's PostgreSQL, Order Service's MySQL, and Inventory Service's MongoDB all use ContainerDb
Each container and database includes its technology in the second-to-last argument of the element declaration
A System_Boundary or Boundary block groups the internal services together
Service-to-service relationship declarations include 'gRPC' in the protocol/technology slot

Service-to-database relationship declarations include a real driver or wire-protocol name in the protocol slot (e.g., 'JDBC', 'libpq', 'MongoDB Wire Protocol') — fabricated combined names like 'SQL/TCP' do not satisfy this
RabbitMQ is represented with ContainerQueue (not plain Container), with separate publish edges from publishers and deliver/consume edges to consumers
Relationship labels are specific verb phrases describing what flows — none of the labels is the bare word 'Uses', 'Reads', 'Writes', or 'Connects'

Service-to-database relationship labels distinguish reads from writes specifically (e.g., 'reads X from' / 'writes Y to' / 'persists X to') rather than collapsing both into 'reads/writes' or 'reads from and writes to'
The diagram includes a title declaration

evals.prompt: I need a quick system context diagram for our analytics platform. It's used by data analysts and product managers. The platform pulls data from our main PostgreSQL production database, from a Salesforce CRM, and from Google Analytics. It also exports reports to a Slack workspace. Can you draw this in ASCII? I want to paste it into a PR description.
evals.expected_output: An ASCII C4 system context diagram showing the analytics platform, its users (data analysts, product managers), and external systems (production database, Salesforce, Google Analytics, Slack), with specific relationship labels and proper ASCII C4 conventions.
expectations: The output is a text-based ASCII diagram, not Mermaid or Structurizr syntax
The analytics platform is shown as the central element
Data analysts and product managers appear as separate people/roles, not collapsed into one
All four external systems are present: production database, Salesforce CRM, Google Analytics, Slack
Relationship labels describe what flows and in which direction — e.g., 'Pulls sales data from', 'Exports reports to', not just 'Uses'
The diagram has a title stating it's a System Context diagram for the analytics platform
Box alignment passes the check_ascii_alignment.py script (run from skill scripts/ directory) with exit code 0
The diagram uses Unicode box-drawing characters (┌─┐│└─┘) consistently — does not mix in ASCII +---+ or other styles
People are notated as parenthesized labels like (Data Analyst), not as multi-line ASCII person figures or full boxes
No protocol or technology details appear in relationship labels — system context is for business intent, not implementation (e.g., no 'JDBC', 'HTTPS', 'REST API' in labels)

Arrow direction matches the verb in the label — if the label says 'Pulls X from', the arrow points from the source TO the platform; if 'Exports to', the arrow points from the platform TO the destination

evals.prompt: Read the code at /Users/ladak/workspace/knowledge/skill-factory and generate a C4 Container diagram of this project. Use ASCII format. Don't ask me questions — figure out the architecture from the code. Show internal containers, external systems, and the people who use it. Save the diagram to a file.
evals.expected_output: An ASCII C4 Container diagram derived from reading the actual skill-factory repo, showing its CLI (skills script), update wrapper, fetch scripts, the docs/output skill directories, and external dependencies (GitHub, claude.com docs, Claude Code, the user's ~/.claude/skills/ directory).
expectations: The output is a text-based ASCII diagram, not Mermaid or Structurizr syntax
The diagram has a title naming this as a Container diagram for the skill-factory (or similar)
The skill author (a person) is shown
Claude Code is shown outside the system boundary as an external system (the framing — consumer of installed skills, authoring environment, etc. — is the author's choice)
GitHub and/or claude.com (or platform.claude.com) appear as external systems for fetching documentation and the skill-creator
The ~/.claude/skills/ filesystem location appears as an external dependency or destination
The skills CLI (./skills) is shown as a container with its responsibility (managing skill installation/symlinking)
The update-docs wrapper or fetch scripts are shown as a container with their responsibility (fetching upstream docs)
The output_skills directory is shown as a container or data store (where authored skills live)
The docs/knowledge directory is shown as a container or data store (where fetched dependencies live)
Relationship labels are specific verb phrases describing what flows — none of the labels is the bare word 'Uses', 'Reads', 'Writes', or 'Connects'

Inter-container relationship declarations include a protocol/technology annotation in brackets when applicable (e.g., [HTTPS], [filesystem], [subprocess], [GitHub REST]) per the skill's container.md rule
The diagram reflects the actual code structure (containers and relationships are grounded in files Claude actually read), not a hallucinated architecture
Box alignment passes the check_ascii_alignment.py script with exit code 0
The diagram uses Unicode box-drawing characters (┌─┐│└─┘) consistently — does not mix in ASCII +---+ or other styles
People are notated as parenthesized labels like (Skill Author), not as multi-line ASCII person figures or full boxes; parentheses are used for people only, not as labels for containers
The diagram is a single coherent figure — containers are not split into disjoint fragments appended below the main figure as a workaround for boundary or alignment constraints
